title: Comparatives: irregular farther/further
question: The shop is ___ than I thought.
explanation: 'Far' has irregular comparatives: far → farther/further. We never say 'more far' or 'farer'.

title: Comparatives: as...as
question: My brother is ___ tall as my father.
explanation: We use 'as...as' to show two things are equal: He is as tall as my father. 'More tall as', 'that tall as', and 'much tall as' are all ungrammatical.

title: Comparatives: negative as...as
question: This test was not ___ difficult as the last one.
explanation: In negative comparisons, we say 'not as...as'. 'Not so much difficult as', 'not more difficult as', and 'not than difficult as' are all ungrammatical.

title: Comparatives: double comparative
question: The days are getting ___.
explanation: We use 'comparative + and + comparative' to show something is increasing: longer and longer. 'More longer' is a double comparative (ungrammatical).

title: Comparatives: double comparative (long adj)
question: The city is becoming ___.
explanation: For long adjectives, we repeat 'more and more': more and more expensive. 'Expensiver' is not a word — long adjectives take 'more', never '-er'.

title: Comparatives: the...the
question: ___ you practice, the better you get.
explanation: We use 'the + comparative, the + comparative' to show connected changes: The more...the better. 'More you practice', 'Much more you practice', and 'The much you practice' are ungrammatical at the start of this construction.

title: Comparatives: irregular worse
question: His handwriting is ___ than mine.
explanation: 'Bad' has an irregular comparative: bad → worse. 'Worser', 'badder', and 'more bad' are not correct.

title: Comparatives: less
question: This movie is ___ exciting than the book.
explanation: 'Less' is the opposite of 'more' for adjective comparisons: less exciting. 'Lesser', 'fewer', and 'littler' do not work before an adjective here.

title: Superlatives: irregular least
question: That was the ___ useful advice I've ever received.
explanation: The superlative of 'little' (in quantity) is 'least': the least useful. 'Less', 'most little', and 'littlest' are ungrammatical here.

title: Superlatives: one of the + plural
question: She is one of the ___ in her class.
explanation: After 'one of the + superlative', we need a plural noun. 'Tallest student' (singular) and 'taller students' (not superlative) are ungrammatical in this frame.

title: First conditional: unless (means 'if not')
question: Which word means 'if not'? You won't pass the exam ___ you study harder.
explanation: 'Unless' means 'if not'. 'Whenever', 'because', and 'although' do not express the condition 'if not'.

title: First conditional: in case (precaution)
question: Choose the phrase meaning 'as a precaution, in case it happens'. Take an umbrella ___ it rains later.
explanation: 'In case' means 'as a precaution for a possible future event'. 'In cases', 'on case', and 'at case' are not valid English phrases.

title: First conditional: as long as (condition)
question: Choose the phrase meaning 'only on the condition that'. You can borrow my car ___ you drive carefully.
explanation: 'As long as' is a fixed conjunction meaning 'on the condition that'. 'As long', 'so long', and 'long as' are not valid conjunctions here.

title: First conditional: provided that
question: Choose the formal phrase meaning 'only if'. I'll lend you the money ___ you pay me back next week.
explanation: 'Provided that' is a fixed conjunction meaning 'only if'. The other forms are ungrammatical as conjunctions introducing a condition.

title: First conditional: unless + negative meaning
question: Which word means 'if she doesn't'? I won't go to the party ___ she calls me first.
explanation: 'Unless' = 'if not'. The other forms are not valid English.

title: First conditional: even if (no matter what)
question: Choose the phrase meaning 'no matter whether'. I'll go for a run ___ it's freezing outside.
explanation: 'Even if' means 'regardless of whether'. 'Even as', 'even that', and 'even of' are not valid conditional conjunctions.

title: First conditional: as long as + don't
question: Choose the phrase meaning 'on the condition that'. He will help you ___ you don't ask too many questions.
explanation: 'As long as' sets a condition. The comparative and 'than' forms are ungrammatical here.

title: First conditional: verb after 'if'
question: If you ___ your homework, I'll let you play outside.
explanation: After 'if' in a first conditional, use the present simple. 'Will finish' is not used in the if-clause. 'Are finish' and 'finishes' (wrong agreement with 'you') are ungrammatical.

title: First conditional: verb after 'unless'
question: ___ he arrives soon, we'll leave without him.
explanation: After 'unless', use a present simple verb. 'Unless will', 'unless to', and 'unlessly' are not valid English.

title: First conditional: 'in case' fixed form
question: I'll pack snacks now ___ we get hungry on the hike.
explanation: 'In case' takes a clause directly: 'in case we get hungry'. 'In the case' and 'in case of' require a noun, not a clause; 'in case that' is not standard.

title: Adverbs: good vs well
question: She sings very ___.
explanation: 'Good' is an adjective; 'well' is the adverb needed after the verb 'sings'. 'Goodly' (archaic) and 'wellly' are not used as adverbs of manner here.

title: Adverbs: hard vs hardly
question: He works very ___ at his job.
explanation: 'Hard' is both an adjective AND an adverb ('work hard'). 'Very harder' is ungrammatical (very + comparative), and 'hardily' / 'hardlier' are not standard here.

title: Adverbs: hard vs hardly (meaning)
question: I ___ know him. We only met once.
explanation: 'Hardly' means 'almost not'. 'Hard', 'harder', and 'hardest' cannot precede 'know him' here; they are ungrammatical in this slot.

title: Adverbs: late vs lately
question: She has been feeling tired ___.
explanation: 'Lately' means recently. 'Latelier', 'more late', and 'latest' are not valid adverbial forms in this slot.

title: Adverbs: position with verbs
question: She ___ forgotten to call me.
explanation: Adverbs go between the auxiliary and the main verb: has probably forgotten. 'Probable' is an adjective and cannot modify a verb; 'probably have' shows wrong agreement with 'she'.

title: Adverbs: position with be
question: The children are ___ quiet today.
explanation: Before an adjective, we need an adverb: unusually quiet. 'Unusual' is an adjective and cannot modify 'quiet'; 'unusualer' is not a word; 'more unusual quiet' is ungrammatical.

title: Adverbs: adjective vs adverb
question: He drove ___ through the storm.
explanation: We need an adverb to describe HOW he drove. 'Careful' is an adjective; 'carefuler' is not a word; 'more careful' is the comparative adjective form and cannot modify the verb 'drove' alone.

title: Adverbs: near vs nearly
question: I ___ missed my flight. I arrived just in time.
explanation: 'Nearly' means 'almost'. 'Nearing', 'nearest', and 'nearer' cannot fill this adverbial slot before 'missed'.

title: Adverbs: fast (no -ly)
question: She runs very ___.
explanation: 'Fast' is both an adjective and an adverb. 'Fastly' is not a word; 'more fast' is ungrammatical (use 'faster'); 'very fastest' is ungrammatical (very + superlative).

title: Adverbs: already position
question: We have ___ eaten dinner.
explanation: 'Already' goes between the auxiliary and the main verb. 'Alreadily', 'more already', and 'alreadys' are not valid words.

title: Gerund vs infinitive: stop + gerund (quit)
question: He stopped ___ because the doctor told him to quit.
explanation: 'Stop + gerund' means to quit an activity. The other forms are ungrammatical after 'stopped' in this slot.

title: Gerund vs infinitive: stop + infinitive (pause)
question: She stopped ___ a coffee on her way to work.
explanation: 'Stop + to-infinitive' means you paused one action in order to do another. 'To buying', 'for buy', and 'buys' are ungrammatical after 'stopped' here.

title: Gerund vs infinitive: remember + gerund (memory)
question: Which sentence means 'I have a memory of locking the door'? I remember ___ the door before I left.
explanation: 'Remember + gerund' expresses a memory of a past action. The other forms are ungrammatical after 'remember' in this slot.

title: Gerund vs infinitive: remember + infinitive (task)
question: Which sentence means 'Don't forget this task'? Please remember ___ the lights when you leave.
explanation: 'Remember + to-infinitive' = a task to do. 'To turning off', 'for turn off', and 'turns off' are ungrammatical after 'remember' here.

title: Gerund vs infinitive: try + gerund (experiment)
question: The room was hot, so I tried ___ the window. It helped a lot!
explanation: 'Try + gerund' means you experiment with a solution. The other forms are ungrammatical after 'tried' in this slot.

title: Gerund vs infinitive: try + infinitive (attempt)
question: I tried ___ the heavy box, but I wasn't strong enough.
explanation: 'Try + to-infinitive' means you attempted something difficult. 'For lifting', 'to lifting', and 'lifts' are ungrammatical after 'tried' here.

title: Gerund vs infinitive: forget + gerund (memory)
question: Which sentence means 'the memory will stay with me'? I'll never forget ___ Paris for the first time.
explanation: 'Forget + gerund' = the memory of a past experience. 'To visiting', 'for visiting', and 'visits' are ungrammatical after 'forget' here.

title: Gerund vs infinitive: forget + infinitive (task)
question: Don't forget ___ milk on the way home.
explanation: 'Forget + to-infinitive' = fail to do a future task. 'To buying', 'for buy', and 'buys' are ungrammatical here.

title: Gerund vs infinitive: enjoy
question: I enjoy ___ in the park on Sundays.
explanation: 'Enjoy' is always followed by a gerund (-ing), never an infinitive or bare verb.

title: Gerund vs infinitive: want
question: She wants ___ a doctor when she grows up.
explanation: 'Want' is always followed by a to-infinitive, never a gerund or bare verb.

title: Too/enough: too + adjective + to
question: This coffee is too hot ___.
explanation: 'Too + adjective + to-infinitive': too hot to drink. The other forms are ungrammatical in this slot.

title: Too/enough: adjective + enough
question: Is the water warm ___ to swim in?
explanation: 'Enough' goes after the adjective: warm enough. 'Enougher', 'enoughly', and 'the enough' are not valid English.

title: Too/enough: enough + noun
question: We don't have enough ___ to finish the project.
explanation: 'Enough' goes directly before an uncountable noun: enough time. 'Of time', 'the time', and 'much time' cannot follow 'enough' — 'enough' already quantifies the noun, so no article or extra quantifier is allowed.

title: Too/enough: enough + to
question: She is old enough ___ a car.
explanation: 'Adjective + enough + to-infinitive': old enough to drive. The other forms are ungrammatical here.

title: Too/enough: too + to
question: He is too young ___ alone at night.
explanation: 'Too + adjective + to-infinitive': too young to go out. The other forms are ungrammatical here.

title: Too/enough: too much/many
question: There are too ___ people in this room.
explanation: 'Too many' is for countable nouns (people). 'Too much', 'mucher', and 'enougher' are ungrammatical here.

title: Too/enough: too much
question: He puts too ___ sugar in his tea.
explanation: 'Too much' is for uncountable nouns (sugar). 'Too many' is for countable nouns; 'mucher' and 'more much' are not valid English.

title: Too/enough: not enough + noun
question: There aren't enough ___ for everyone.
explanation: 'Not enough + plural countable noun': not enough chairs. 'Of chairs', 'chair' (singular), and 'chairses' are ungrammatical here.

title: Too/enough: enough position
question: The box is light ___ for me to carry.
explanation: After an adjective with 'for me to carry', only 'enough' fits. 'Light too for me to carry', 'light so for me to carry', and 'light very for me to carry' are all ungrammatical (these words precede the adjective).

title: Too/enough: too...to vs enough...to
question: She spoke too quickly for me ___. Can you repeat it?
explanation: 'Too + adverb + for someone + to-infinitive': too quickly for me to understand. The other forms are ungrammatical here.
